summary:
The most distinctive sign often associated with diagnosing mandibulofacial dysostoses like Treacher Collins Syndrome includes a notch or defect present near the outside corners of both eyes due to colobomata affecting these areas. While structural abnormalities such as vertical maxillary hypoplasia are also important indicators, they may not be immediately apparent without specialized imaging techniques; hence focusing initially on visible ocular anomalies can expedite accurate diagnosis critical early intervention steps. To clarify based solely upon provided context:

The most prominent diagnostic indicator typically seen in patients diagnosed with Mandibulofacial Dysostosis involves an external eye deformity characterized by absent tissue forming partway down from each side’s outer eyecorner ('coloboma'). Although skeletal malformations exist too, recognizing this specific facial anomaly allows quicker identification necessary before further assessments commence promptly addressing potential complications arising during infancy/childhood stages when treatment windows open up effectively. Agent2 concludes that "normal vision" combined with presence of coloboma should ideally characterize mandibulofacial dysostosis rather than just mentioning any one symptom alone because multiple While structural abnormalities such as vertical maxillary hypoplasia are also important indicators, they may not be immediately apparent without specialized imaging techniques; hence focusing initially on visible ocular anomalies can expedite accurate diagnosis critical early intervention steps. To clarify based solely upon provided context:

Although skeletal malformations exist too, recognizing this specific facial anomaly allows quicker identification necessary before further assessments commence promptly addressing potential complications arising during infancy/childhood stages when treatment windows open up effectively.
